data.title: HSBC Had St. James's Place at Reduce
Delta Air Lines Regional Plane Crashes and Flips Over. What to Know. — Barrons.com
data.description:
Al Root

A Delta Air Lines flight operated by a regional jet subsidiary dramatically flipped over on landing at Pearson International Airport in Toronto, Canada.

The Delta Connection flight 4819 was operated by Delta subsidiary Endeavor Air and originated from Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport. The incident happened at about 2:15 p.m. Eastern time on Monday.

"Initial reports indicate there are no fatalities, and 18 customers with injuries have been transported to area hospitals," reads part of a statement from Delta. "Our primary focus is taking care of those impacted."

The aircraft involved was a Bombardier CRJ 900. Bombardier introduced the 900 model in the early 2000s, but the company sold its regional jet business to Mitsubishi Heavy Industries in 2020.

The Delta incident comes weeks after an American Airlines regional flight crashed with an Army helicopter, tragically killing everyone involved.

Coming into Tuesday's trading, American Airlines stock was down about 8% since the collision. The S&P 500 was up about 1% over the same span.

The plane involved in the American Airlines crash was a Bombardier CRJ 700. There hasn't been any information putting the plane at fault in either incident, and both investigations are ongoing.

The National Transportation Safety Board held a Feb. 14 briefing about the Jan. 29 American Airlines crash. There are open questions about the altitude of the helicopter and if the helicopter crew received air traffic updates.

Write to Al Root at allen.root@dowjones.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Southwest Airlines to Slash 1,750 Corporate, Leadership Roles in Restructuring
data.description:
Southwest Airlines said Monday it will reduce its workforce by 1,750 mosrly corporate and leadership employees inas part of a restructuring.

The layoffs represent about 15% of corporate jobs and include senior leadership and director posts, including vice presidents, and will be mostly complete by H2 2025.

The company said it estimates savings from the layoffs will total $210 million in 2025 and $300 million in 2026, excluding expected one-time Q1 2025 charges of $60 million to $80 million in severance payments and post-employment benefits.

President and Chief Executive Officer Bob Jordan said the changes will make the airline "into a leaner, faster, and more agile organization."

data.title: Alkemy Capital Investments Unit Kicks Off Feed Study for British Lithium Hydroxide Refinery
data.description:
Alkemy Capital Investments' subsidiary Tees Valley Lithium, or TVL, launched the front-end engineering design study for its lithium hydroxide refinery in Teesside, UK.

TVL is also advancing due diligence with investors and preparations for the development phase under the newly appointed Chief Executive Officer Vikki Jeckell, the investment company said Tuesday.

data.title: Capgemini earnings lack momentum, shares eye worst day in 5 yrs
data.description:
** Shares in Capgemini fall 7.5% on the French IT consultancy firm's "conservative" outlook following "mixed" FY results

** Capgemini is guiding for -2% to +2% constant currency revenue growth in 2025, after its 2024 sales dipped 2% in 2024 to 22.10 billion euros ($23.12 billion), even if q/q the revenue growth improved slightly

** J.P.Morgan says that "2025 outlook implies negative organic growth and H1 growth to remain subdued"

** Stifel says the update is "mixed" and Capgemini's guidance is "understandably conservative"

** "The group lacks momentum in 2024 and potentially in 2025, particularly in view of the discretionary nature of spending, its exposure to the auto sector and to Europe," MidCap Partners says

** Shares are on track for worst day in nearly five years, among the worst performers on STOXX 600 and at the bottom of France's blue-chip index CAC 40

($1 = 0.9557 euros)

data.title: Direct Line Cut to Hold by Peel Hunt
Malayan Banking CFO Quits
data.description:
Malayan Banking Chief Financial Officer Puan Khalijah Binti Ismail left the company following an internal inquiry conducted by the bank.

The lender has begun identifying her replacement, according to a Monday filing with Bursa Malaysia.

Encik Malique Firdauz Ahmad Sidique will assume the position of acting group CFO.

data.title: Monad to roll out a public testnet on Feb. 19
data.description:
High-performance EVM blockchain Monad said its public testnet would be rolled out on Feb. 19, a critical milestone for the project’s development.

The testnet has been in development for two years and serves as a testing environment for developers and users to experiment with the platform’s features before the mainnet launch.

This follows the rollout of a closed testnet in late 2024. Plans for a tentative mainnet launch are expected later in 2025.

Monad aims to function as a high-throughput network, claiming to support 10,000 transactions per second and 1-second block times. It leverages technologies like parallel execution and features a custom database called MonadDb.

It's also fully compatible with Ethereum Virtual Machine (EVM) bytecode, making it easier for developers familiar with Ethereum to transition their applications.

Monad Labs, the developer behind the blockchain, was co-founded by former Jump Trading developers. It has raised $225 million in funding in a round led by Paradigm.

Disclaimer: The Block is an independent media outlet that delivers news, research, and data. As of November 2023, Foresight Ventures is a majority investor of The Block. Foresight Ventures invests in other companies in the crypto space. Crypto exchange Bitget is an anchor LP for Foresight Ventures. The Block continues to operate independently to deliver objective, impactful, and timely information about the crypto industry. Here are our current financial disclosures.

data.title: Delta Air Lines' Regional Jet Crashes at Toronto Pearson Airport, Injuring 18
data.description:
A Delta Air Lines regional jet flipped over while landing at Toronto Pearson Airport on Monday amid strong winds following a snowstorm, multiple media outlets reported, citing local officials.

The reports said the crash injured 18 of the 80 people onboard, including three critically, one of whom was a child.

The flight, DL4819, arrived from Minneapolis-St. Paul International Airport, the report said, adding the CRJ900 aircraft operated by Endeavor Air suffered significant damage, with one wing torn off.

Canadian authorities are investigating the cause of the crash, which was not yet known, according to the reports.

The 16-year-old CRJ900 was made by Canada's Bombardier and powered by GE Aerospace (GE) engines.

data.title: Walmart Earnings, Fed Minutes, Housing Sales: What to Watch This Week — WSJ
data.description:
By WSJ Staff

Investors will get new clues about the health of the consumer and housing market with earnings and economic data this week.

Here's the economic and corporate news to watch:

Today

  • Earnings: Baidu, Medtronic, Occidental Petroleum, Toll Brothers, Arista Networks
  • Fed speaker: Vice Chair for Supervision Michael Barr is slated to speak in New York on AI in the economy.

Tomorrow

  • Earnings: Analog Devices, Carvana, Etsy
  • Data: Housing-starts data for January are due at 8:30 a.m. ET.
  • The Fed releases minutes at 2 p.m. of its January policy-setting meeting, at which the central bank held rates steady.
  • Fed speaker: Vice Chair Philip Jefferson is due to speak at 5 p.m. on household balance sheets.

Thursday

  • Earnings: A big day for corporate results, headlined by Walmart. The retailer's stock has been on a tear of late as the company hones its e-commerce strategy.
  • Alibaba, Wayfair, Hasbro, Block and Rivian are also reporting.
  • Fed speakers: Michael Barr is scheduled to discuss supervision and regulation at 2:30 p.m., in what could be his last public comments before he gives up his supervisory role at the end of the month.
  • Fed governor Adriana Kugler is also slated to speak: about inflation, at 5 p.m.

Friday

  • Data: Existing home-sales data for January are due at 10 a.m.

Saturday

  • Reports: Berkshire Hathaway is expected to release its annual report, with a shareholder letter from Chairman and Chief Executive Warren Buffett, around 8 a.m.

This item is part of a Wall Street Journal live coverage event. The full stream can be found by searching P/WSJL (WSJ Live Coverage).

data.title: Vestas Could Deliver in 2025 After Strong Finish to 2024 — Market Talk
data.description: Vestas had a strong finish to 2024 and RBC Capital Markets is cautiously optimistic about its ability to deliver in 2025. RBC says the underlying historical profitability of Vestas' service business, combined with management's action plan and peer performance, provides confidence that the business can recover. RBC also expects installations in Germany to increase in 2025. With its present valuation still pointing to a 40%-50% sector discount and implying no margin uplift from 2025, the bank sees an attractive risk-reward. It expects 2025 margins near the top end of the guided 4%-7% adjusted EBIT margin range. RBC reiterates its outperform rating and trims its price target to 154 Danish kroner from 156.00 kroner. Shares rise 1.8% to 98.58 kroner. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

data.title: Holiday Inn owner IHG to return $1.1 billion to shareholders after 2024 beat
data.description:
Key points:
  • 2024 RevPAR growth of 3% beats expectations
  • Forecast higher-than-expected interest expenses
  • Shares down 1.3%

Holiday Inn owner IHG is returning more than $1.1 billion to shareholders in 2025 and buying European urban hotel brand Ruby for $116 million, it said on Tuesday, after annual room revenue beat expectations.

IHG, which also owns Crowne Plaza and Six Senses hotels, reported growth of 3% in annual room revenue, above market expectations, boosted by a pick-up in demand in the United States and despite weakness in China.

Analysts had expected average revenue per available room (RevPAR), a key industry metric, to grow 2.6% for the year ended December 31, 2024, a company-compiled consensus showed.

CEO Elie Maalouf said he planned to expand the Ruby brand to the United States and Asia. Currently the business operates 20 hotels in European cities.

"We would expect this (Ruby) brand to compete with Hilton’s Motto and CitzenM, both successful brands globally," analysts at Bernstein said in a note.

In the United States, its largest market, IHG reported a RevPAR growth of 1.7% for the year. In China, RevPAR fell 4.8%.

IHG kept its medium-term targets, but projected 2025 adjusted interest expense to range between $190 million and $205 million, above analysts' consensus estimate of $174 million.

Shares in the company, which scaled all-time highs last week, were down 1.2% in early trade, with Jefferies analysts saying the worse-than-expected interest expense forecast could drag profit estimates.

Peers Marriott International and Hilton Worldwide had forecast a downbeat 2025, hurt by poor performance at hotels in Greater China, while Hyatt Hotels reported a less than stellar fourth quarter last week.

On Tuesday IHG reported annual operating profit in line with market expectations.

It launched a new $900-million share buyback programme and proposed a 10% increase in its annual dividend, taking shareholder returns this year to more than $1.1 billion.

($1=0.9555 euros)

data.title: Telefónica Picks Nokia to Boost Network in Spain
data.description:
Spanish telecommunications giant Telefónica selected Nokia to roll out its 4G and 5G network operations in Spain.

The deal covers the implementation of the Finnish telecommunications group's Packet Core technology for Telefónica's low-latency services, according to a Tuesday release.

data.title: UK Shares Waver on Tuesday
data.description:
The FTSE 100 was little changed on Tuesday after gaining 0.4% in the previous session, as investors assessed jobs data and corporate earnings.

Wage growth excluding bonuses hit its fastest pace since last April, reinforcing inflation concerns that could delay Bank of England rate cuts and keep government borrowing costs high.

Wages rose 5.9% in Q4, matching forecasts, while the rate including bonuses climbed to 6%, exceeding expectations.

Unemployment held at 4.4%, below the anticipated 4.5%.

In corporate news, Antofagasta gained over 1% after reporting stronger-than-expected profits, while Anglo American rose 0.6% after agreeing to sell its nickel business for up to $500 million.

Meanwhile, Intercontinental Hotels fell over 1% despite beating revenue estimates and raising its dividend by 10%.

data.title: European Bond Yields Continue to Rise, Ukraine War In Focus
data.description:
Bond yields in Europe continued to rise, with the benchmark Germany’s 10-year bond yield reaching 2.5%, the highest level in nearly three weeks, as traders assessed prospects of increased defense spending and higher bond issuance across the Eurozone and the potential impact on inflation and interest rates.

With the US signaling plans to scale back support for Ukraine and pursuing negotiations with Russia to end the war, European governments convened in Paris to discuss alternative support strategies.

However, the informal meeting concluded without concrete measures.

Strengthening defense and aiding Ukraine could cost Europe's major economies an estimated $3.1 trillion over the next decade.

On the monetary policy front, the European Central Bank is expected to cut interest rates by 25 bps at each of the next three meetings, with the deposit rate possibly falling below 2% by 2026.

data.title: Hanwha Aerospace Shares Surge, Leading South Korean Defense Stocks' Rally
data.description:
By Kwanwoo Jun

Hanwha Aerospace's shares surged to a new high Tuesday, leading a rally in South Korean defense stocks.

The South Korean company's stock rose 11% to close at a record 643,000 won, equivalent to $445.82, outperforming the benchmark Kospi's 0.6% gain.

The manufacturer of self-propelled howitzers and aircraft engines under Hanwha Group stood out among local peers. Shares of guided-missile supplier LIG Nex1 and fighter-jet maker Korea Aerospace Industries rose 9.1% and 7.4%, respectively.

The business environment continues to be favorable for South Korean defense companies, Seoul-based LS Securities analyst J.H. Choi said in a research note Tuesday, with global defense spending likely to rise as the U.S. is expected to gradually disengage from international conflicts under President Trump.

Choi picked Hanwha, which posted strong fourth-quarter earnings on brisk contract wins at home and abroad, as the likely best performer among local defense shares in the near term. He initiated coverage of the stock with a buy rating and a target price of 710,000 won.

Hanwha is forecast to increase its 2025 shipments of self-propelled howitzers, aircraft engines and parts for fighter jets and long-range surface-to-air missiles, Choi said.

The company unveiled its L-SAM system during a defense exhibition in Abu Dhabi this week for the first time to secure more contract wins in the Middle East market.

Choi forecasts Hanwha's revenues to more than double to 29.7 trillion won this year, with operating profit projected to jump 55% to 2.8 trillion won.

Write to Kwanwoo Jun at kwanwoo.jun@wsj.com

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0840 GMT - A less stringent approach to borrowing could allow Europe to invest more in defense while ironing out national-level discrepancies, Capital Economics' Jack Allen-Reynolds says in a note. The continent is gearing up to increase defense spending in response to shifting geopolitics, especially regarding Russia and the U.S. While national governments could borrow more to fund the extra spending, many--including France and Italy--already suffer from overstretched budgets, and others may not play ball, Allen-Reynolds says. Borrowing at the EU level would prove cheaper and more cost-effective, he says. While there is often resistance to extra borrowing, "the bloc's response to Covid-19 in 2020 shows that attitudes can change very quickly in times of crisis." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0833 GMT - Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise as investors lower their expectations of Bank of England interest-rate cuts following stronger-than-expected labor-market data. "The jobs market holds up better than expected, generating strong wage growth that will keep the [BOE] monetary policy committee cautious," Pantheon Macroeconomics' Rob Wood says in a note. Average wage growth excluding bonuses in the three months to December accelerated to 5.9% from 5.5% previously. Unemployment held steady at 4.4%, below the consensus forecast of 4.5% by economists in a WSJ poll. The 10-year gilt yield climbs 4 basis points to 4.563% , Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

0832 GMT - The impact of the forthcoming rise in U.K. employer national-insurance contributions and the national living wage could prompt the Bank of England to speed up interest-rate cuts in the second half of the year, Abrdn's Luke Bartholomew says in a note. For now, rate cuts will remain gradual after data Tuesday showed average pay growth picked up to 5.9% at the end of 2024, he says. However, the big test will be how the labor market reacts to the changes announced in the government'sautumn budget. There are "clear signs" that hiring intentions have cooled in anticipation, he says. "If growth remains very sluggish there is a clear path to more rapid rate cutting in the second half of the year." (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

0817 GMT - The U.K. jobs market is due some loosening, Deutsche Bank's Sanjay Raja tells investors in a note. Unemployment was stable at 4.4% at the end of last year and average pay growth picked up pace to 5.9%, figures show Tuesday, pointing to a robust labor market despite signs of weakness in separate surveys. But an increase in employer taxes due to come into effect this spring will come at a price, and pay growth will also likely slow following a spike from a rise in minimum wages also due this spring, Raja says. "The labor market is creaking but not cracking," he says. "Some slack is emerging, but we aren't seeing a worrying spiral in unemployment - just yet."(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0815 GMT - The dollar rises as its recent downward correction has likely run its course in the absence of tangible negative drivers, ING analyst Francesco Pesole says in a note. Markets will remain focused on any developments on U.S.-Russia talks over the Ukraine war, he says. Barring any major breakthrough, the "optimistic push and relative upbeat risk sentiment may stall or fade in the next couple of days and the dollar can continue to recover some ground." The DXY dollar index rises 0.3% to 106.915 after falling to a two-month low of 106.566 on Friday. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - U.K. businesses need a confidence boost, says Alex Hall-Chen at lobby group the Institute of Directors after data showed vacancies fell again in the three months to January. U.K. wage growth accelerated at the end of last year and unemployment remained stable, figures show Tuesday, but the drop in vacancies shows firms have little optimism to take on new employees, Hall-Chen says."The cumulative impact of recent employment reforms, coupled with upcoming increases to employer National Insurance Contributions and the National Living Wage, is significantly weakening the business case for hiring," he tells clients in a note, calling on business-friendly action from the government in London.(joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

0736 GMT - March is likely to be a key test for China's economy and markets, Citi analysts write in a note. The rally in Chinese equities so far is a tech-led, sentiment-driven dynamism without significant changes in economic fundamentals, they add. The National People's Congress is scheduled to kick off on March 5 and is expected deliver the details of policy thinking, they say, adding that they the meeting is seen to be a mark-to-market one. The brokerage maintains its realistic expectations for a fiscal expansion of CNY2.5 trillion with trade-in subsidies doubling to CNY300 billion this year. "We tend to look for a solid start, with potential upside coming from export front-loading," the analysts write. A broad-based macro economy recovery marked with reflation may not be at the corner yet, they add. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0727 GMT - Sterling rises to a two-week high against the euro and trims losses versus the dollar after data showed the U.K. unemployment rate unexpectedly held steady and wages accelerated in the three months to December. The jobless rate remained at 4.4% compared with the 4.5% expected in a WSJ survey of economists. Average earnings, excluding bonuses, rose 5.9% on year after a 5.6% rise in the previous three-month period, as expected. This is "incompatible with a sustainable return to the Bank of England's 2% inflation target over the medium term," Pepperstone strategist Michael Brown says in a note. EUR/GBP falls to a low of 0.8293 after the data from 0.8303 beforehand. GBP/USD rises to 1.2623 from 1.2601. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0721 GMT - The main story in the European fixed income market is the increase in public spending on defense and aid to Ukraine, says Danske Bank Research's August Hyldgaard in a note. The European Union is looking into various options to fund the increased spending through either joint financing or suspending the EU fiscal rules, similar to that during the Covid-19 crisis, the analyst says. "The response in the bond market was higher yields across the EGBs [eurozone government bonds], but with tighter spreads between periphery and semi-core EGBs relative to core EGBs," he says. This means that there isn't much flight to quality despite the higher yields and the pressure on fiscal policy, he says. The 10-year Bund yield rises 0.5 basis point to 2.488%, according to LSEG data. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0713 GMT - Eurozone government bonds experienced some weakness on Monday, with yields rising as markets price in U.S.-EU relations souring, according to Pepperstone's Michael Brown in a note. Bond markets are also pricing in a risk premium associated with the upcoming German elections, the senior research strategist says. Higher government bond yields also signal the potential of higher investor insurance as European nations attempt to finance a sharp rise in defense spending, Brown says. The eurozone 10-year bond yields rose by 4 to 7.5 basis points across the board on Monday, according to Tradeweb data. Eurozone bond investors will be closely watching for the release of Germany's ZEW sentiment survey on Tuesday, while bond issuance will come from Germany and Finland. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0659 GMT - The German 10-year Bund yields are likely to rise to 2.75% by the year-end, according to DZ Bank Research's Birgit Henseler. Germany could pursue an expansionary fiscal policy as a result of structural reforms and expenditure on defense, the analyst says in a note. "This should drive up 10-year yields," she says. The expected increase in U.S. Treasury yields will also lead to upward movement in Bund yields, she says. However, the 10-year Bund yields could move sideways around 2.50% in the next six months before rising in the second half of 2025, she says. On Monday, the 10-year Bund yield rose 6 basis points to 2.48%, according to Tradeweb. (emese.bartha@wsj.com)

0657 GMT - Thailand's exports could face downside risks from rising U.S. tariff threats, according to DBS economist Chua Han Teng in a note. He explains that the country's exports are particularly vulnerable to U.S. reciprocal tariffs due to the significant tariff differential between Thailand and the U.S. across a wide range of products. However, Chua points out that strong performance in Thai exports was supported by manufacturing shipments, especially electronics, driven by the global tech upcycle. Despite these risks, Chua believes that resilient external demand is likely to support Thai exports at least through the early part of this year, Chua says.(amanda.lee@wsj.com)

data.title: EU Could Take on Defense Borrowing Together — Market Talk
data.description: A less stringent approach to borrowing could allow Europe to invest more in defense while ironing out national-level discrepancies, Capital Economics' Jack Allen-Reynolds says in a note. The continent is gearing up to increase defense spending in response to shifting geopolitics, especially regarding Russia and the U.S. While national governments could borrow more to fund the extra spending, many--including France and Italy--already suffer from overstretched budgets, and others may not play ball, Allen-Reynolds says. Borrowing at the EU level would prove cheaper and more cost-effective, he says. While there is often resistance to extra borrowing, "the bloc's response to Covid-19 in 2020 shows that attitudes can change very quickly in times of crisis." (joshua.kirby@wsj.com; @joshualeokirby)

data.title: N. Macedonia’s airports see 9% y/y drop in Jan passengers
data.description:
SKOPJE (N. Macedonia), February 18 (SeeNews) - North Macedonia’s two international airports, in Skopje and Ohrid, served a total of 221,101 passengers in January, down 9.1% compared with the same month a year earlier, Aeroports de Paris (ADP) said.

However, passenger traffic at the two airports in January 2025 was 137.8% higher than in January 2019 before the pandemic, ADP said in a statement published on Monday.

Aircraft movements at the two airports came in at 1,828 in January, some 5.3% lower compared with January 2024.

ADP owns 46% of Turkey’s TAV Airports, the company operating the airports in Skopje and Ohrid.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

0831 GMT - Capgemini's guidance for 2025 looks conservative, Jefferies analysts writes in a note. The French consulting and technology group's full-year results were marginally ahead of expectations at all levels, the analysts say. However, the outlook for 2025 feels cautious, they add. "Having misjudged the speed of market recovery in 2024, we can understand the rationale for starting 2025 on a more conservative footing," the U.S. bank says. Shares are down 7.7% at 171.01 euros. ( najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0828 GMT - Antofagasta's 2024 performance was broadly in line at an operating level, while the net cash figure and dividends came in ahead of expectations, RBC Capital Markets analysts say in a note. The Chilean copper miner reported Ebitda of $3.4 billion, 2% ahead of RBC's estimate due to lower-than-expected spend on exploration and corporate costs, they say. The final dividend of 31.4 cents a share was nearly 20% higher than RBC's forecast, they add. With the copper price hikes overdone, the stock could be dragged by the commodity's return to $4.00 per pound expected for 2025, the analysts add. Shares are up 0.8% at 18.50 pounds. (michael.susin@wsj.com)

0827 GMT - Hong Kong shares ended higher, with the Hang Seng Index rising 1.6% to 22976.81 and the Hang Seng Tech Index gaining 2.5%. Chinese President Xi's meeting with several tech leaders on Monday was seen as a positive signal for the sector. Among the major stocks, Alibaba was 3.4% higher, and Xiaomi added 7.2%, supported by news that its new car model was eligible for a purchase tax waiver. Baidu gained 0.4% ahead of its 4Q earnings. Decliners included Longfor Group, which fell 1.2%, and Trip.com, which slipped 1.1%. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0816 GMT - Tencent is likely to benefit from the integration of DeepSeek with its messaging app WeChat and its intelligent workstation product ima.copilot, Great Wall Securities analyst Hou Bin says in a research note. DeepSeek's open-source nature and cost efficiency have cut developing costs, and the collaboration between the two may become an example for the AI industry in the future, the analyst says. "As a domestic user ecosystem giant, [Tencent] has closely followed the AI ​​trend, and its overall ecosystem has gained momentum," the analyst says. Meanwhile, Tencent's game business has resumed an upward trend with growth gradually accelerating, and profitability has continued to improve, the analyst adds. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0810 GMT - The key to UniCredit's pursuit of Banco BPM is paying the right price, JP Morgan says in a research note. Banco BPM lifted its offer price for asset manager Anima, a deal it decided to go ahead with despite not knowing if it would cost it 40 or 290 basis points of capital. This depends on whether it can use the Danish compromise but the ECB's regulatory decision on this has been delayed. UniCredit's threat to drop out shows M&A price discipline and willingness to keep the offer price low, analysts write. They doubt UniCredit will pull its bid given its long-standing interest in BPM. If the Danish compromise is applied, UniCredit could sweeten its offer for BPM with 2.3 billion euros in cash, but if it isn't, the Italian bank is unlikely to pay a premium for its smaller peer, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0809 GMT - BT's Openreach revenue will likely decline in the near term and continue out to 2030, Citi analysts write in a note. The fall in consumer price index combined with continued broadband base decline support the forecast for lower revenue, the analysts say. This questions the U.K. telecommunications company's ability to achieve its guidance for 3 billion pounds of normalized free cash flow by the end of the decade, they add. The sustainability of BT's consumer pricing dynamic in the long term also raises concerns, they say. "Consensus is over-optimistic in expecting restructuring specific item costs to reduce significantly in the next few years." The U.S. bank downgrades its recommendation on the stock to sell from buy and cuts its target price to 112 pence from 200 pence. Shares are down 4.6% at 144.45 pence. (najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

0803 GMT - Singapore Technologies Engineering's order wins are poised to support growth beyond 2024, RHB Research's Shekhar Jaiswal says in a research report. Earlier this month, the technology and engineering group unveiled a strong S$4.3 billion of orders won for 4Q 2024, the analyst notes. This winning streak has persisted in 2025, as its commercial aerospace business has recently secured maintenance, repair and overhaul contracts for aircraft engines with two major Middle Eastern operators, the analyst says. The company's earnings outlook remains positive, says RHB, which expects CAGR of roughly 15% for 2023-2026. RHB has a buy rating and target price of S$5.20 on the shares, which are 1.2% higher at S$5.06. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0800 GMT - China's recent AI-driven stock rally may signal broader improvements in the country's venture capital ecosystem, Pepperstone research strategist Dilin Wu says in a note. DeepSeek's success has proven that AI startups can deliver tech breakthroughs even with limited computing power, the analyst notes. Its success could open new technical pathways for the sector and provide early-stage Chinese tech companies with access to more funding and resources, Wu says. This shift has energized the AI sector and spurred growth across the tech supply chain, such as in chip and AI hardware makers, the analyst adds. China's recent policy tailwinds to support high-quality tech companies should further fuel innovation, Wu adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

0758 GMT - Mercedes-Benz will hold a capital markets day along with the presentation of its 2024 results, and management will be graded on the credibility of achieving the targets that will be announced at the event, Bernstein analysts write. "Having flunked with its recent battery-electric vehicles, Mercedes has a high hurdle to overcome." The bank expects to learn more details of the company's cost-cutting program, as well as a new weather map to describe its expected performance and prevailing market conditions. The bank expects management to address the poor share price performance and shareholder return plans, as well as offer an update on its plans for China. Bernstein will also look for a strategic update regarding electrification and mix. Shares closed at 61.50 euros. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0739 GMT - The FTSE 100 is expected to open six points higher, or 0.1%, according to IG. The index closed 35 points higher, or 0.4%, to 8768 points on Monday. Data Tuesday showed the U.K. unemployment rate held at 4.4% while average earnings, excluding bonuses, accelerated to 5.9% year-on-year in the three months to December. Economists in a WSJ survey expected the unemployment rate to rise to 4.5% while wage growth was in line with forecasts. "Overall, today's data release provides little evidence that the Bank of England will deviate from its current gradual approach to interest rate cuts," Capital Economics economist Ashley Webb says in a note. (renae.dyer@wsj.com)

0726 GMT - Aztech Global's order book may recover in 2026, CGS International's William Tng says in a research report. A key customer for the electronics-product manufacturer may still need some time to digest excess inventories before a potential order rebound in 2026, the analyst says. The company will also likely continue efforts to achieve customer diversification, Tng says, noting the company has been engaging potential and current customers in new product activities. The brokerage upgrades the stock to add from reduce as a recent order-book slowdown has probably been priced in. CGS raises the target price to S$0.82 from S$0.78. Shares are 5.8% higher at S$0.73. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - Chinese shares closed lower positive sentiment over President Xi's meeting with tech CEOs and the recent Chinese AI hype took a back seat. Investors are watching for the "Two Sessions" meeting in early March to evaluate Beijing's economic growth plan for this year. Semiconductor and technology stocks led the gains. Will Semiconductor closed 5.4% higher and Shengyi Technology rose 4.6%. Meanwhile consumption and media stocks led losses, with Mango Excellent Media down 6.9%. Oppein Home lost 6.0% and Focus Media Information Technology fell 3.9%. The benchmark Shanghai Composite Index closed 0.9% lower at 3324.49 and the Shenzhen Composite Index fell 2.0%. The ChiNext Price Index ended 2.0% lower.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

data.title: Capgemini's Outlook Looks Conservative — Market Talk
data.description: Capgemini's guidance for 2025 looks conservative, Jefferies analysts writes in a note. The French consulting and technology group's full-year results were marginally ahead of expectations at all levels, the analysts say. However, the outlook for 2025 feels cautious, they add. "Having misjudged the speed of market recovery in 2024, we can understand the rationale for starting 2025 on a more conservative footing," the U.S. bank says. Shares are down 7.7% at 171.01 euros. ( najat.kantouar@wsj.com)

data.title: Gilt Yields Rise After Stronger-Than-Forecast U.K. Jobs, Wages Data — Market Talk
data.description: Yields on U.K. government bonds, or gilts, rise as investors lower their expectations of Bank of England interest-rate cuts following stronger-than-expected labor-market data. "The jobs market holds up better than expected, generating strong wage growth that will keep the [BOE] monetary policy committee cautious," Pantheon Macroeconomics' Rob Wood says in a note. Average wage growth excluding bonuses in the three months to December accelerated to 5.9% from 5.5% previously. Unemployment held steady at 4.4%, below the consensus forecast of 4.5% by economists in a WSJ poll. The 10-year gilt yield climbs 4 basis points to 4.563% , Tradeweb data show. (miriam.mukuru@wsj.com)

data.title: BOE Rate Cuts Could Speed Up in Second Half Due to Higher Employer Tax — Market Talk
data.description: The impact of the forthcoming rise in U.K. employer national-insurance contributions and the national living wage could prompt the Bank of England to speed up interest-rate cuts in the second half of the year, Abrdn's Luke Bartholomew says in a note. For now, rate cuts will remain gradual after data Tuesday showed average pay growth picked up to 5.9% at the end of 2024, he says. However, the big test will be how the labor market reacts to the changes announced in the government'sautumn budget. There are "clear signs" that hiring intentions have cooled in anticipation, he says. "If growth remains very sluggish there is a clear path to more rapid rate cutting in the second half of the year." (jessica.fleetham@wsj.com)

data.title: Europe stocks continue record run on defence sector boost
data.description:
European shares rose to an all-time high on Tuesday as defence stocks continued to climb on the likelihood of increased military spending in the region, although a Capgemini-led drop in IT stocks capped the gains.

The pan-European STOXX 600 index rose 0.2% to a record of 555.42 points, with the aerospace and defence index (.SXPARO) jumping 1%.

The sector surged 4.6% on Monday, its biggest one-day climb since Russia invaded Ukraine in February 2022, after European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen said the Commission will propose exempting defense from EU limits on government spending.

Defence stocks continued to gain on Tuesday, with Italy's Leonardo rising 1.4%, Sweden's Saab AB up 1% and Britain's BAE Systems advancing 0.5%.

German arms maker Rheinmetall ticked up 0.2%, while Thyssenkrupp , which is looking to spin off its warship division, advanced 2.7% after soaring nearly 20% on Monday.

On the flip side, Capgemini slid 6.2% after the French IT consulting giant reported a 2% annual decline in sales, though it surpassed expectations.

IHG , the owner of Holiday Inn, lost 1.3% following the announcement of its 2024 results.

Antofagasta gained nearly 1% after the miner's annual core profit rose 11%.
